w:r.w:t: ER Diagrams - Physical Schema - Indexing & Partitioning - RLS - Migrations
Vol. 1 Business Architecture; Vol. 2 Application Architecture (bounded contexts, UML); Vol. 3 Technical Architecture (PostgreSQL, shared-schema + RLS multi-tenancy decision)
Vol. 5 Integration Architecture; Vol. 6 Security Architecture; Vol. 7 Infrastructure & Deployment; Vol. 8 Testing & QA; Vol. 9 UI/UX & API Design

This volume turns the domain model (Vol. 2 Section 8 UML), the bounded-context ownership map (Vol. 2 Section 3.2), and the PostgreSQL/shared-schema/RLS decision (Vol. 3 Section 2.2, 11) into a physical data architecture: entity-relationship diagrams per bounded context, physical table definitions for the highest-traffic entities, indexing and partitioning strategy, the concrete Row-Level Security implementation, and the schema migration and data retention strategy.

Consistent with Vol. 3 Section 3 ("a module may only import another module's public export surface"), the same rule applies at the data layer: each bounded context owns a PostgreSQL schema (namespace) and is the only code allowed to write to its own tables. Other contexts read via the owning context's application-layer API (Vol. 2 Section 11), never via direct SQL joins across schema boundaries. Where a report genuinely needs cross-context joins, it queries the OpenSearch read-model (Vol. 3 Section 2.6) built from published domain events, not the transactional schemas directly.
Every table carries tenant_id and is protected by Row-Level Security
tenant_id UUID NOT NULL on every table; RLS policy per table (Section 7)
ledger.journal_entries and ledger.journal_lines have no UPDATE/DELETE grants for the application role; corrections are reversing entries (Vol. 1 Principle P4)
Primary keys are UUIDv7 (time-ordered UUIDs), not sequential integers
Avoids leaking tenant record-count/growth-rate via sequential IDs; time-ordering keeps index locality reasonable despite UUID randomness
Money is stored as fixed-point NUMERIC, never floating point
NUMERIC(19,4) for amounts, paired with a currency CHAR(3) column (Vol. 3 Section 4.2 CS-03 Money type)
Every table has created_at, updated_at, and created_by/updated_by audit columns
Populated by application layer, indexed for audit-trail queries (Vol. 1 BC-20)
Soft-delete only where the business allows deletion; financial records are never hard- or soft-deleted, only voided/reversed
is_voided flag + reversing entries for financial tables; deleted_at nullable timestamp for genuinely deletable master data (e.g. draft-only records)
Foreign keys always include tenant_id in the join condition (composite FK or enforced via RLS + application check)
Prevents cross-tenant relationship bugs even if a query bypasses RLS accidentally (defense in depth)

ER diagrams are grouped by bounded context/schema, matching Section 1.2's ownership rule. Cross-schema references shown as dashed relationships are logical (enforced at the application layer + eventual consistency via domain events), not physical foreign keys, per Section 1.2.
ER diagram - payables and inventory schemas

Full DDL for all ~120 tables across 16 schemas belongs in the migration source tree, not this document. This section gives representative DDL for the three highest-risk tables (per Vol. 2 Section 8's flagged critical flows) to fix the concrete column-level conventions every other table must follow.
id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v7(),
tenant_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES identity_tenant.tenants(id),
journal_entry_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES ledger.journal_entries(id),
account_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES ledger.accounts(id),
created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
ALTER TABLE ledger.journal_lines ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
-- No UPDATE or DELETE grants issued to the application role (Principle D2)
id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v7(),
tenant_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES identity_tenant.tenants(id),
customer_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES receivables.customers(id),
is_voided boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
ALTER TABLE receivables.invoices ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v7(),
is_active boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT true,
created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
-- tenants table itself has no tenant_id (it IS the tenant boundary) and no RLS policy;
-- access to it is restricted at the application-role level instead (Vol.6 will formalize).
Account ledger/statement queries (most common read pattern)
Fetch all lines for a given journal entry
Enforce human-readable numbering uniqueness per tenant
(tenant_id, id) composite or tenant_id as leading column on every index
Every query is tenant-scoped (RLS-driven); leading tenant_id keeps index scans tenant-local even under a shared table

General rule: tenant_id is always the leading column of every composite index, since RLS means every query implicitly filters on it - a non-leading tenant_id would force the planner to scan across tenants before filtering.
Only tables with unbounded per-tenant growth and high aggregate write volume across all tenants are partitioned; small reference tables are not, to avoid unnecessary operational complexity.
RANGE partition by created_at, monthly, with pg_partman automating new-partition creation and old-partition archival

Highest write-volume table across all tenants combined; monthly partitions keep index size and vacuum time bounded; old partitions can be moved to cheaper storage tiers per Section 10 retention policy
High volume but lower than journal_lines; quarterly balances partition count against per-partition size
Continuous high-volume ingestion from bank feeds across all tenants
RANGE partition by created_at, monthly, with aggressive archival after the compliance retention window (Section 10)
Write-heavy, rarely read except for audits; safe to move to cold storage quickly

Explicitly NOT partitioned at Release 1: identity_tenant.tenants, ledger.accounts, receivables.customers, inventory.items - these are reference/master data with bounded per-tenant row counts (Section 11 classification).
Implements the Vol. 3 Section 11 decision. Every tenant-scoped table gets an identical policy shape, generated by a migration helper rather than hand-written per table, to guarantee consistency.
ALTER TABLE <schema>.<table> ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
-- Application connection sets this once per request (Vol.3 Section 11.2):
No BYPASSRLS role in application connections
The application's PostgreSQL role never has BYPASSRLS or superuser; only a narrowly-scoped migration/ops role does, used exclusively for schema changes, never application traffic
If app.current_tenant_id is not set on a connection, the policy evaluates to false for every row (no accidental full-table access) rather than failing open

Because RLS relies on a session-local setting, the connection pooler (e.g. PgBouncer) must run in session mode for tenant-scoped connections, or the tenant context must be re-set on every checkout in transaction-pooling mode - this is a hard requirement for Vol. 7's infrastructure sizing
CI includes a cross-tenant-leakage test suite (Vol. 8) that attempts to read tenant B's data while scoped to tenant A and asserts zero rows returned, run against every table with RLS enabled
Paired with a char(3) currency column; never numeric without explicit precision, never float/double
varchar with a CHECK constraint (not native PG enum)
varchar chosen over native enum so new statuses can be added without a blocking ALTER TYPE migration
Used only where the business concept has no time component
created_at, updated_at timestamptz; created_by, updated_by uuid
Present on every table; updated_at maintained by a trigger, not application code, to guarantee correctness
Only on genuinely deletable master data (Section 2, D6); never on financial transaction tables
Backs Vol.1 BC-17 custom fields; indexed with a GIN index where queried

Schema migrations are managed with a code-first migration tool (e.g. node-pg-migrate or Prisma Migrate, consistent with the TypeScript stack in Vol. 3) with every migration checked into the same monorepo as the module it belongs to (Vol. 3 Section 3.2).

Because the platform uses a single shared schema per environment (not per-tenant databases, Vol. 3 Section 11), a migration runs once per environment and applies to all tenants simultaneously - this makes migration safety a first-class operational concern, not an edge case.

All migrations must be backward-compatible with the currently-deployed application version for at least one release cycle (expand/contract pattern): add new columns as nullable or with defaults first, deploy code that writes to both old and new shapes, backfill, then drop the old column in a later migration.
Destructive migrations (DROP COLUMN, DROP TABLE) require a two-step process: mark deprecated in one release, physically remove in the next, with a manual sign-off gate (Vol. 8 CI pipeline).
A[Release N: Add new nullable column/table] --> B[Deploy app code that writes both old + new shape]
B --> C[Backfill existing rows - batched, rate-limited background job]
C --> D[Deploy app code that reads only from new shape]
D --> E[Release N+1: Migration drops old column/table]

Because these tables are partitioned (Section 6), schema changes are applied per-partition in small batches rather than as a single blocking ALTER TABLE across the entire (potentially billions-of-rows) table - avoiding long-held locks that would stall the append-only ledger writes financial correctness depends on.
Retained indefinitely online for the tenant's active subscription; statutory minimum 7 years even after tenant offboarding (varies by jurisdiction - Vol.5/6 to confirm per-country requirements)
Partitions older than 24 months moved to cheaper storage tier (Section 6); still queryable, just slower
Same as ledger - these are the source records for statutory retention
Minimum 7 years per common financial-audit regulatory expectation (to be confirmed per market in Vol.6)
Aggressive partition archival to cold object storage after 12 months
3 years online, then archived (post-reconciliation, raw import data has diminishing operational value)
TTL-bound, not part of durable retention

Soft-deactivate tenant immediately; hard-delete only after the statutory retention window and an explicit data-deletion request (GDPR right-to-erasure interacts with financial retention law - Vol.6 to resolve the precedence)
11. Master Data vs. Transactional Data Classification
This classification drives both the partitioning decisions (Section 6) and the caching strategy (Vol. 3 Section 7: master data is cache-friendly, transactional data generally is not).
tenants, accounts (chart of accounts), customers, vendors, items, tax_rates, roles
Low write frequency, bounded row count per tenant, high read frequency, cache-friendly (Vol.3 Section 7.1)
journal_entries, journal_lines, invoices, invoice_line_items, bills, payments, bank_transactions, stock_adjustments
High write frequency, unbounded growth over time, partitioned (Section 6), generally not cached beyond short-TTL aggregates
OpenSearch report indices (Vol.3 Section 2.6), dashboard aggregate cache
Rebuildable from transactional data + events; not a source of truth, never backed up independently of its source events
Vol. 2 Section 3.2 Bounded Context Catalog; Vol. 3 Section 3 module boundary rule
Confirm statutory retention periods per target country (US/India/EU per Vol.1 Section 13.1 assumption) so Section 10's retention table can be finalized with exact durations rather than placeholders.

Confirm whether PgBouncer session-mode pooling (Section 7 caveat) is acceptable from a connection-scaling standpoint, or whether an alternative RLS-compatible pooling strategy is needed - this materially affects Vol. 7 infrastructure sizing.
Confirm migration tooling preference (node-pg-migrate vs Prisma Migrate vs other) if the team has existing expertise to weigh in, since this affects Section 9's tooling choice.

On approval, Phase 5 begins: Integration Architecture (Volume 5) - detailed design for banking/bank-feed integration, payment gateway integration, tax/e-filing integration, and e-commerce channel sync, all consuming the schemas and event contracts fixed in this volume and Volume 2.
